_comment: Golden cases for the LIVE screening eval (app/evals/live_screening.py). Each case is a real SYNTHETIC applicant (exact slice from the pool), run through the REAL production screening prompt+model. Screening produces a LIST of integrity flags; the grade is per-CATEGORY: metadata.expected.fires = categories that MUST appear; metadata.expected.absent = categories that must NOT (the over-reach guards — flagging benign things is the costly error since flags gate eligibility). A clean applicant has empty fires and any flag fails it. given.fields (normalized form fields) + given.essays (keyed by the real form-question column) are what the screening prompt receives; metadata is harness-only. See docs/eval-case-schema.md.
judge_background: Screening reviews an applicant's fields and essays for INFORMATIONAL integrity concerns — never disqualifying, only surfaced for a human to review. Flag genuine concerns such as: internal inconsistencies between fields/essays; placeholder or fake contact details (keyboard-mash emails, obviously bogus phone numbers); placeholder names (e.g. a child named 'TBD'); and pet descriptions that violate the co-op pet policy (at most 1 dog; at most 1 cat; no other/exotic pets — the pets field is free text, so account for negation like 'no pets' and unclear phrasing). Flag ONLY real concerns: a benign detail (e.g. a child with a different surname, or a name/email that simply differ in form) must NOT be flagged — a false flag is the costly error.

metadata.note: Two dogs and a cat against a 1-dog/1-cat policy — a clear pet_policy flag.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 59 (Roberto Vega) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We camp and kayak whenever we can and pack out more trash than we bring. We value showing up for the people around us, not just for ourselves.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I'm a heavy-duty mechanic and can keep the boiler room, shared tools, and grounds equipment running. I'll take the toughest maintenance shifts and teach others basic repair skills.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: I spent six years on the maintenance committee of my last building, scheduling repairs and saving us thousands by doing the work in-house.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I believe housing works better when neighbours share responsibility, and I'd bring practical maintenance skills to the mix. I'd pitch in on budgeting and make sure the numbers stay honest.

metadata.note: An exotic/undomesticated pet (a "velociraptor") against a dogs/cats-only policy — a pet_policy flag.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 6 (Quinn Kim) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.

fields.pets_text: We have a velociraptor named Kevin. He's leash-trained and very polite.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: Outside of work I'm a keen cyclist and weekend volunteer at the food bank. Fairness and follow-through are the things we care about most.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I'm a software developer and could build and maintain a simple booking and maintenance-request system. My partner is reliable about showing up for work hours and weekend cleanups.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: We volunteered for three years with a community garden collective, which taught us how shared responsibility actually plays out. It taught me to write things down so the next volunteer isn't lost.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: Co-op living matches how we already try to live — shared effort, honest meetings, and looking out for each other. I'd keep the shared spaces cared for and the noticeboard up to date.

metadata.note: OVER-REACH GUARD: an email local-part that differs from the applicant name is common (old address, shared account) — must NOT raise a fake_contact flag. Production flagged this in run 2; the judge labelled it flag_unsupported.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 88 (Jules Abara) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.

fields.pets_text: Not applicable, we are not pet owners

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We grow vegetables on a shared allotment and swap the surplus with neighbours. We value showing up for the people around us, not just for ourselves.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I've chaired a strata maintenance committee and can schedule trades, get quotes, and track warranties. Between us we can cover a regular maintenance shift without much supervision.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: We haven't lived in a co-op before, but we've been active in our building's tenants' association. I came to value members who simply do what they said they would.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: We want somewhere secure to raise our family and would gladly help with whatever the co-op needs. I'd bring my trade skills to the jobs that would otherwise need a contractor. A couple we garden with are longtime members, and they're the ones who encouraged us to apply because they think we'd slot in well.

metadata.note: OVER-REACH GUARD: children with surnames differing from the adults is normal (blended families, kept names) — must NOT raise an internal_inconsistency flag.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 27 (Sloan Obrien) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We're long-distance runners and help marshal local charity races. We'd rather build something together than rent in isolation.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I'm a software developer and could build and maintain a simple booking and maintenance-request system. We're organized and good at following through on the boring but necessary tasks.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: I served two terms on a strata council and know how to read a budget and sit through a long agenda without losing the room. I saw firsthand how a few reliable people keep the whole place afloat.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I'm drawn to the democratic side of co-ops — I'd rather help govern a place than be a tenant who just complains. I'd help mediate the small frictions before they turn into big ones.

metadata.note: A wholly unremarkable applicant — expect ZERO flags. Any flag is a false positive.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 1 (Avery Nguyen) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: I read widely, run a small book club, and bake far too much bread. We'd rather build something together than rent in isolation.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: Honestly, neither of us is handy at all. We can't fix things, we don't garden, and we'd be useless on a maintenance shift. We're happy to pay someone, but hands-on work isn't us.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: I was on the membership committee at a food co-op and handled new-member orientation and the work-shift schedule. I got comfortable chasing quotes, tracking repairs, and closing the loop.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I think deeply about what it means to share a home with others, and I write and reflect on community as a way of life. I'd bring warmth, good conversation, and a genuine ethic of care.

metadata.note: A keyboard-mash placeholder email (asdf@asdf.asdf) is a clear fake-contact concern.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 13 (Peyton Mehta) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.
metadata.label_rationale: FLAG_SUPPORTED: 'asdf@asdf.asdf' is an unmistakable placeholder/keyboard-mash, not a real address — the cited evidence is the concern itself.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We organize the annual block party and its cleanup crew. Community and accountability are values we take seriously.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I manage bookkeeping for a small business, so I can help keep the co-op's budget, reconcile accounts, and prep for audits. We're both comfortable being on the on-call list for small emergencies.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: I grew up in a housing co-op in Burnaby, so I understand the rhythm of meetings, work hours, and shared decisions. It gave me patience for consensus and an eye for what really needs fixing.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I value mutual aid over going it alone, and I'd contribute by mentoring new members and keeping our systems documented. I'd help mediate the small frictions before they turn into big ones.

metadata.note: Essay says "devoted parent of two" but the form lists four children — a real, evidenced inconsistency.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 8 (Reese Anderson) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.
metadata.label_rationale: FLAG_SUPPORTED: a stated 'parent of two' directly contradicts a form listing four children — a real, evidenced internal inconsistency worth a screener's review.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: Reese Anderson is a dedicated accountant and a devoted parent of two. The Anderson family values hard work, tidiness, and giving back to their community. They look forward to contributing.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I'm a graphic designer and can produce the newsletter, signage, and the move-in welcome packet. We learn quickly and don't mind being shown how a building's systems work.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: We lived in a co-housing community in the Kootenays for five years and took turns on the maintenance and finance teams. It showed me how shared budgets and fair work-shares actually get set.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I want to age in a place where people check on each other, and I can offer steady hands for upkeep and finances. I'd pitch in on budgeting and make sure the numbers stay honest.

metadata.note: pet_policy (two cats vs max one) is the hard, agreed fire. internal_inconsistency on the skills essay is a CONTESTED axis, deliberately left out of BOTH fires and absent so it's ungraded either way: the same essay ('we don't really have any handy skills… that said, I'm a certified electrician') was read by one production run as self-deprecating modesty (don't flag) and by another — after the prompt broadened contradictions to cover within-essay — as a direct self-contradiction (flag). Both are defensible tone-vs-fact calls; stability on that flag is the signal, not its direction. Screening is per-category, so only the contested AXIS is left ungraded, not the whole case.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 12 (Riley Singh) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.
metadata.label_rationale: pet_policy FIRES (two cats exceed the max of one) — both runs agree. internal_inconsistency is genuinely contested (modesty-self-corrected vs. factual self-contradiction), so it is neither required nor forbidden here; the case grades only on the pet_policy breach.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We restore old bicycles and donate them to newcomers to the city. We try to live simply and share what we have.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: Honestly we don't really have any handy skills to offer. That said, I'm a certified electrician, my partner does drywall and tiling, and we both have first-aid training, so we'll figure it out.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: We lived in a co-housing community in the Kootenays for five years and took turns on the maintenance and finance teams. It gave me patience for consensus and an eye for what really needs fixing.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: Honestly? We're just so done with crummy landlords and surprise rent hikes. A co-op sounds amazing — we'd totally pitch in, show up for work days, the whole deal. Fingers crossed!

metadata.note: A rabbit against a dogs/cats-only policy (no other/exotic pets) — a pet_policy flag.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 38 (Hiroshi Tanaka) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.
metadata.label_rationale: FLAG_SUPPORTED: the policy explicitly bars 'other/exotic pets', and a rabbit is neither a dog nor a cat, so it falls under 'other' — a genuine violation. Note the policy line is included in the case (fidelity): without it, the quote alone ('at most 1 dog and 1 cat') could look like it doesn't cover rabbits; with it, the flag is clearly warranted.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We foster rescue animals and are active in the local pet-adoption network. We believe a good home is one you help take care of.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I've done HR and conflict mediation for a decade and can help the co-op resolve disputes fairly. We're both comfortable being on the on-call list for small emergencies.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: We lived in a co-housing community in the Kootenays for five years and took turns on the maintenance and finance teams. I learned to spot the work that goes unnoticed until it's not done.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: I want my kids to grow up knowing their neighbours and seeing adults solve problems together, and I'll pull my weight to make that real. I'd mentor newer members and document how things actually work here.

metadata.note: A child with first and last name both "TBD" (age 5) is a genuine placeholder worth surfacing.
metadata.source: synthetic pool, application id 75 (Jennifer Ross) — an exact slice of a real synthetic record.
metadata.label_rationale: FLAG_SUPPORTED: 'TBD' for both names of a named age-5 child is a genuine placeholder worth surfacing — the evidence is the concern.

essays.Please introduce yourself and your famil: We're into board games and host a monthly potluck for friends. Fairness and follow-through are the things we care about most.
essays.Please tell us about any skills you and: I'm a transit mechanic and can service the shared tools, bikes, and any small engines on site. We learn quickly and don't mind being shown how a building's systems work.
essays.Please tell us about any previous co-op: I sat on the board of a childcare co-op and learned how to keep volunteers engaged without burning them out. It showed me how shared budgets and fair work-shares actually get set.
essays.Describe why you want to live in a co-op: We want to put down roots and give back, whether that's chairing a committee or just keeping the boiler room tidy. In return I'd take on a committee seat and keep our records straight.
